solvers.name: Gurobi Optimizer
solvers.academic_details: Free full-featured academic license for faculty and students at degree-granting institutions. No variable/constraint limits. Requires .edu domain or institutional verification. Off-campus use needs VPN or cloud license.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact sales. No public price list. Estimated ~$12,000+/year per named user. Volume discounts available.
solvers.description: Industry-leading commercial solver for LP, MIP, and QP. Known for speed, reliability, and excellent documentation. Used widely in industry and academia.
when_to_use: Speed is critical and budget is available
Large-scale MIP (100K+ variables)
You need best-in-class LP/MIP performance
Academic research (free license)
when_not_to_use: No budget and not in academia
MINLP or global optimization (use BARON or SCIP)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Free academic license is full-featured BUT tied to institutional domain. Expired students lose access immediately. No public commercial pricing — you must contact sales. Withdrew from Mittelmann benchmarks in August 2024, so independent benchmark data is limited post-2024.

quick_start.code:
import gurobipy as gp
from gurobipy import GRB

m = gp.Model()
x = m.addVar(name="x")
m.setObjective(x, GRB.MAXIMIZE)
m.addConstr(x <= 10)
m.optimize()
print(f"Optimal: {x.X}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Consistently fastest on LP and MIP benchmarks. Withdrew from Mittelmann benchmarks Aug 2024. Historical data shows leading performance on MIPLIB, Netlib LP.

hidden_costs: Server deployment requires separate Compute Server license
Annual renewal — prices may increase
Per-named-user — each developer needs a seat

migration_from.cplex: Similar API paradigm. Most Pyomo/JuMP models work with a one-line solver swap.
migration_from.highs: Drop-in for LP/MIP via Pyomo or PuLP. Expect significant speedup on large instances.

migration_to.highs: If budget is the issue, HiGHS handles LP/MIP competitively up to medium scale.
migration_to.cplex: Similar commercial tier. Compare pricing — IBM may offer better enterprise deals.

community_quotes.text: Best MIP solver bar none, but the pricing is brutal for small companies.
The academic license is amazing — full-featured, no variable limits. But the moment you graduate, it's gone.

solvers.name: IBM ILOG CPLEX
solvers.academic_details: Free via IBM Academic Initiative. Requires institutional email and annual renewal. Some features now behind IBM Cloud (watsonx) paywall. DOcplex Python API free for academic use.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact IBM. Enterprise pricing. Often bundled with IBM Decision Optimization suite.
solvers.description: Mature commercial solver from IBM. Strong LP/MIP performance. Part of the larger IBM Decision Optimization ecosystem. Long history in operations research.
when_to_use: Enterprise environment already using IBM stack
Need CP Optimizer (companion product) for constraint programming
Large-scale LP/MIP with IBM support contracts
when_not_to_use: Prefer open ecosystem without vendor lock-in
solvers.licensing_gotcha: IBM has slowed CPLEX release cadence significantly. Last major release was May 2024. Pricing through IBM sales only — expect enterprise-level pricing. Academic license available through IBM Academic Initiative but process can take weeks. If starting fresh, evaluate alternatives.

quick_start.code:
from docplex.mp.model import Model

m = Model(name='example')
x = m.continuous_var(name='x', ub=10)
m.maximize(x)
m.solve()
print(f"Optimal: {x.solution_value}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Competitive with Gurobi on LP/MIP. Strong on large-scale industrial problems. CP Optimizer (separate product) is Tier 1 for constraint programming.

hidden_costs: Complex enterprise procurement process
IBM Cloud Pak bundling may be required
Slow release cadence — last major update May 2024

migration_from.gurobi: Similar performance tier. Pyomo/JuMP models swap in one line.

migration_to.gurobi: Most common CPLEX-to-Gurobi migration path. Similar API concepts.

community_quotes.text: CPLEX is solid but IBM's sales process is painful. We waited 3 weeks for a quote.

solvers.name: HiGHS
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. MIT license. No restrictions whatsoever.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Free. MIT license allows unrestricted commercial use.
solvers.description: High-performance open-source solver for LP, MIP, and QP. Fastest open-source LP solver. Actively developed with frequent releases. Interior point method (HiPO) added in 2026.
when_to_use: Need a free solver with no licensing restrictions
LP problems of any scale (competitive with commercial)
Medium-scale MIP (closing gap with commercial solvers)
Python, Julia, C++, or Rust projects
Teaching and prototyping
when_not_to_use: Very large MIP requiring absolute fastest solve time (Gurobi still faster)
MINLP or global optimization (use SCIP or BARON)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: None. Truly free for everything. MIT license. The only 'gotcha' is performance — genuinely slower than Gurobi on hard MIP instances, though the gap is narrowing with every release.

quick_start.code:
import highspy

h = highspy.Highs()
h.setOptionValue('output_flag', False)
x = h.addVariable(0, 10)
h.changeObjectiveSense(highspy.ObjSense.kMaximize)
h.changeColCost(x, 1.0)
h.run()
print(f"Optimal: {h.getInfoValue('objective_function_value')[1]}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Fastest open-source LP solver on Mittelmann benchmarks. MIP performance improving rapidly — ~2-5x slower than Gurobi on MIPLIB benchmark set, but gap closing with each release. HiPO interior point method added 2026.

migration_from.cbc: Similar API concepts through PuLP. Swap solver name in 1 line.
migration_from.glpk: Direct replacement for LP. Install highspy, change solver reference.
migration_from.gurobi: HiGHS handles LP/MIP but is slower on large instances. Good for cost reduction.

migration_to.gurobi: If HiGHS is too slow on large MIP, Gurobi is the natural upgrade. Same modeling tools (Pyomo, PuLP) work with both.

community_quotes.text: We migrated from CBC and got 3x speedup for free. HiGHS is the real deal for open-source LP/MIP.
HiGHS is now the default LP solver in SciPy. That's a massive endorsement.

solvers.recent_changes:
The highlight of v1.14 is the extension of the HiPO interior point LP
solver to handle convex QPs.

## Code changes

Prompted by [#2821](https://github.com/ERGO-Code/HiGHS/issues/2821),
the trea
github_recent_prs: Updated build for external dependencies (mods-latest)
Fix C-heap memory leaks in solver state cleanup
Updated build for external dependencies
Reduce shift size in reduced-cost fixing
Allow slightly asymmetric square Hessians, and revert to using `HighsHashTable` (mainly) In `assessMatrix`

solvers.name: SCIP
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source since 2022 (Apache 2.0). Previously was ZIB Academic License (research-only). Now unrestricted.
solvers.description: Non-commercial solver for MIP, MINLP, and Constraint Integer Programming. Part of the SCIP Optimization Suite (includes SoPlex LP solver, ZIMPL modeling language, GCG decomposition framework). Strong on MINLP. Extensible via plugins.
when_to_use: MINLP or nonlinear integer programming
Research requiring solver customization (plugin architecture)
Need to solve Constraint Integer Programs (CIP)
Academic projects requiring extensibility
when_not_to_use: Need fastest MIP performance (Gurobi is faster)
Need many language bindings (Python and C/C++ only)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Switched from ZIB Academic License to Apache 2.0 in 2022. Many online resources still say SCIP is 'non-commercial only' — this is outdated. SCIP 9.0+ is fully open source. SoPlex (LP solver component) is also Apache 2.0.

quick_start.code:
from pyscipopt import Model

m = Model()
x = m.addVar("x", ub=10)
m.setObjective(x, "maximize")
m.optimize()
print(f"Optimal: {m.getObjVal()}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Best open-source option for MINLP. On pure MIP, slower than Gurobi/CPLEX/HiGHS. SCIP 10.0 added rational MIP solving, improved symmetry handling, new heuristics.

hidden_costs: Steeper learning curve than LP-only solvers
PySCIPOpt wrapper has its own version compatibility

migration_from.baron: SCIP handles MINLP with open-source license. Different solver strategies — test on your instances.

migration_to.gurobi: For pure MIP, Gurobi will be faster. SCIP's strength is MINLP and extensibility.

community_quotes.text: SCIP is the Swiss Army knife of optimization. Not the fastest on pure MIP, but unmatched for extensibility and MINLP.

solvers.recent_changes:
Fixed bugs
----------

- an error is now produced if the emphasis settings in parameter files are wrong
- propagate focus lower bound to child nodes to avoid outdated global lower bound
- set sma
github_recent_prs: Allow documentation builds when testing is turned off
Fix endianness issue in dtoa.cpp
Fix two bugs in SCIPcreateConsBasicSOCNonlinear
Fix typo in nauty.h pragma directive intrinsic
make bundled CppAD atomic_base threadsafe

solvers.name: Google OR-Tools
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. Apache 2.0. No restrictions.
solvers.description: Google's OR toolkit. Excellent CP-SAT solver for constraint programming. Includes specialized solvers for vehicle routing (routing library), scheduling, and assignment. GLOP for LP. MIP via PDLP or external solvers.
when_to_use: Constraint programming problems (CP-SAT is excellent)
Vehicle routing with complex constraints
Scheduling and assignment problems
Need a free, well-maintained toolkit from a major vendor
when_not_to_use: Pure LP/MIP performance (GLOP/PDLP not competitive with Gurobi/HiGHS)
Need algebraic modeling language (use Pyomo or JuMP instead)
Need R, Julia, or MATLAB support
solvers.licensing_gotcha: OR-Tools bundles multiple solvers: CP-SAT (constraint programming — excellent), GLOP (LP — decent), PDLP (LP — experimental), routing library (VRP — strong). People sometimes expect MIP performance competitive with Gurobi and get disappointed — OR-Tools' MIP solver is NOT its strength. Use CP-SAT for constraint problems, routing library for VRP, and an external solver (HiGHS/Gurobi) for pure MIP.

quick_start.code:
from ortools.sat.python import cp_model

model = cp_model.CpModel()
x = model.new_int_var(0, 10, 'x')
y = model.new_int_var(0, 10, 'y')
model.add(x + y <= 15)
model.maximize(x + 2 * y)

solver = cp_model.CpSolver()
status = solver.solve(model)
if status == cp_model.OPTIMAL:
    print(f"x={solver.value(x)}, y={solver.value(y)}")

compatible_modeling_tools: Direct API only (no Pyomo/JuMP integration for CP-SAT)
solvers.benchmark_notes: CP-SAT is competitive with IBM CP Optimizer on many constraint benchmarks. MIP solver (GLOP/PDLP) is NOT competitive with Gurobi, CPLEX, or HiGHS. Use CP-SAT for constraint problems, external solver for MIP.

hidden_costs: Google may deprecate or change API without long notice
Not a traditional solver — different API paradigm

migration_to.gurobi: For pure MIP performance, use OR-Tools's pywraplp with Gurobi backend.
migration_to.highs: OR-Tools wraps HiGHS for LP/MIP. Good free alternative to its internal GLOP.

community_quotes.text: CP-SAT is criminally underrated. For scheduling and assignment problems, it often beats MIP formulations.

solvers.recent_changes:

solvers.name: COIN-OR CBC
solvers.description: Mature open-source MIP solver from the COIN-OR project. Default solver in PuLP. Stable but development has slowed significantly. Being overtaken by HiGHS in performance.
when_to_use: Default solver in PuLP (no extra install needed)
Legacy projects already using CBC
Simple MIP problems where speed isn't critical
when_not_to_use: New projects (HiGHS is faster and more actively maintained)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: CBC is stable but development has slowed. Last significant release was 2024. HiGHS has surpassed CBC in performance on most benchmarks. If you're starting a new project, HiGHS is the better open-source choice.

quick_start.install: pip install pulp  # CBC included as default solver
quick_start.code:
from pulp import *

prob = LpProblem('example', LpMaximize)
x = LpVariable('x', 0, 10)
prob += x
prob.solve()  # Uses CBC by default
print(f"Optimal: {value(x)}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Outperformed by HiGHS on most LP and MIP benchmarks. Still functional and stable, but no longer competitive with actively developed alternatives.

migration_from.glpk: CBC is generally faster than GLPK. Same PuLP interface.

migration_to.highs: HiGHS is faster than CBC on most LP/MIP instances. Same PuLP interface — one-line swap.
migration_to.gurobi: Significant speedup. PuLP/Pyomo models work with both.

community_quotes.text: CBC is fine for prototyping and teaching, but if you need production speed, move to HiGHS or Gurobi.

solvers.recent_changes:
## What's Changed
* Fix compiler warnings by @svigerske in https://github.com/coin-or/Cbc/pull/503
* Fix SOS2 branching by @svigerske in https://github.com/coin-or/Cbc/pull/504
* Fix clang warnings
github_recent_prs: Update windows-ci for action update for Node.js 24 to set up msvc for stable
Update windows-ci for action update for Node.js 24 to set up msvc
Update linux-ci for macos and all ci for action updates for Node.js for stable
Update linux-ci for macos and all ci for action updates for Node.js
Output facelift: structured tabular output for all solver phases

solvers.name: FICO Xpress
solvers.academic_details: Free academic license available. Community edition with limited size.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact FICO sales. Enterprise pricing similar to Gurobi/CPLEX.
solvers.description: Commercial solver with broad problem type coverage including MINLP and CP. Includes Mosel modeling language. Strong in financial services industry.
when_to_use: Need MINLP in a commercial solver
Financial services optimization
Already using FICO ecosystem
when_not_to_use: Simple LP/MIP (HiGHS is free and competitive)
Python-first workflow (Gurobi has better Python integration)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Academic license has size limits in community edition. Full academic license requires application. Mosel modeling language is proprietary — models written in Mosel are locked to Xpress.

quick_start.code:
import xpress as xp

x = xp.var(ub=10)
prob = xp.problem()
prob.addVariable(x)
prob.setObjective(x, sense=xp.maximize)
prob.solve()
print(f"Optimal: {prob.getObjVal()}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Competitive with Gurobi and CPLEX on LP/MIP. One of three solvers consistently in Tier 1 on Mittelmann benchmarks. Also supports MINLP via global solver (since 2023).

hidden_costs: Often bundled with FICO platform — standalone pricing unclear
Community edition available with variable limits

solvers.name: MOSEK
solvers.academic_details: Free full-featured academic license. Generous — covers personal academic use with no variable limits.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact MOSEK. Pricing is more accessible than Gurobi/CPLEX for conic optimization users.
solvers.description: Specialized in conic optimization (SOCP, SDP). Industry standard for semidefinite programming. Also handles LP, QP, and MIP. Preferred solver for CVXPY users.
when_to_use: Semidefinite programming (SDP) — the standard solver
Second-order cone programming (SOCP)
Conic optimization problems
CVXPY users (MOSEK is the recommended backend)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Academic license is genuinely generous — full-featured, personal use. Commercial pricing is reasonable for conic optimization users. MOSEK 11 added new MATLAB API. Interior point solver now exploits symmetries.

quick_start.code:
import mosek
import sys

with mosek.Task() as task:
    task.appendvars(1)
    task.putvarbound(0, mosek.boundkey.ra, 0.0, 10.0)
    task.putcj(0, 1.0)
    task.putobjsense(mosek.objsense.maximize)
    task.optimize()
    result = task.getxx(mosek.soltype.bas)
    print(f"Optimal: {result[0]}")

solvers.benchmark_notes: Industry standard for SDP and SOCP. Competitive on LP. MIP solver improving but not main strength. MOSEK 11 brought performance improvements for interior-point and branch-and-bound.

hidden_costs: Pricing tiers based on problem size
Free personal academic license available

solvers.name: BARON
solvers.academic_details: Free academic license available with size limits (30 variables for standalone). Full version through GAMS academic license.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Via GAMS or AMPL licensing. Contact vendor.
solvers.description: Leading global optimization solver for MINLP. Guarantees global optimality (not just local optima). Performance doubles approximately every major release.
when_to_use: MINLP requiring guaranteed global optimality
Non-convex optimization problems
Chemical engineering, process optimization
when_not_to_use: Large-scale problems (global optimization is inherently slower)
Need direct Python API (requires GAMS/AMPL layer)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Standalone academic license limited to 30 variables. Full academic access requires GAMS academic license. Not available as a standalone Python package — accessed through GAMS or AMPL interfaces.

quick_start.code:
# Accessed via GAMS or AMPL, not direct Python API
# Example using Pyomo + BARON (requires GAMS):
import pyomo.environ as pyo
m = pyo.ConcreteModel()
m.x = pyo.Var(bounds=(0, 10))
m.obj = pyo.Objective(expr=m.x**2 - 4*m.x, sense=pyo.minimize)
pyo.SolverFactory('baron').solve(m)

solvers.benchmark_notes: Fastest and most robust MINLP solver according to published benchmarks. 9 updates in 2025 with improvements in presolve, convexity identification, and relaxation strategies.

hidden_costs: Requires GAMS or AMPL license separately
Total cost = BARON + modeling language license

solvers.name: Hexaly (formerly LocalSolver)
solvers.description: Hybrid solver combining exact and heuristic methods. Uses its own modeling paradigm (LSP language). Good for very large-scale combinatorial problems where traditional MIP is too slow. Rebranded from LocalSolver in 2023.
when_to_use: Very large combinatorial problems where MIP is too slow
Need fast feasible solutions (not necessarily optimal)
Scheduling and routing at industrial scale
when_not_to_use: Need to integrate with Pyomo/JuMP ecosystem
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Rebranded from LocalSolver in 2023. Old 'LocalSolver' API deprecated as of Hexaly 14.0 (mid-2025). Uses its own LSP modeling language — not compatible with Pyomo/JuMP/AMPL ecosystem. Results are heuristic (good feasible solutions, not proven optimal).

quick_start.install: # Download from hexaly.com, requires license key
quick_start.code:
import hexaly.optimizer as gp

with gp.HexalyOptimizer() as optimizer:
    model = optimizer.model
    x = model.int(0, 10)
    model.maximize(x)
    model.close()
    optimizer.param.time_limit = 5
    optimizer.solve()

solvers.benchmark_notes: Not directly comparable to MIP solvers. Designed for very large problems where finding good feasible solutions fast matters more than proving optimality. Competitive on large JSSP benchmarks.

hidden_costs: Proprietary modeling language (LSP) — steep learning curve
Smaller community than mainstream solvers

solvers.name: GLPK
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Free, but GPL requires derivative works to be open source.
solvers.description: GNU Linear Programming Kit. Mature, stable, but development has effectively stopped (last release Dec 2020). Historically significant but now outperformed by HiGHS and CBC.
when_to_use: Legacy projects already using GLPK
GPL-compatible projects requiring GPL solver
Very simple LP problems
solvers.licensing_gotcha: GPL v3 — derivative works must also be GPL. This is a deal-breaker for many commercial projects. Last release was December 2020. Effectively abandoned. Use HiGHS instead for any new project.

quick_start.install:
# Available through package managers:
# Ubuntu: apt install glpk-utils libglpk-dev
# macOS: brew install glpk
quick_start.code:
# Typically accessed via PuLP or Pyomo, not directly
from pulp import *
prob = LpProblem('example', LpMaximize)
x = LpVariable('x', 0, 10)
prob += x
prob.solve(GLPK_CMD(msg=0))

solvers.benchmark_notes: Slowest mainstream solver. Outperformed by HiGHS, CBC, and all commercial solvers. No active development.

hidden_costs: GPL license — may require open-sourcing your code if distributing
No active development since 2020

solvers.name: CVXPY
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. Apache 2.0. No restrictions.
solvers.description: Python-embedded modeling language for convex optimization, widely used in ML and finance
when_to_use: Convex optimization with disciplined convex programming (DCP)
Rapid prototyping of optimization models in Python
ML and finance applications (portfolio optimization, SVM, etc.)
Need to switch solvers easily (MOSEK, SCS, ECOS, OSQP backends)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: CVXPY itself is free, but the best backend solvers may not be. MOSEK (recommended for SDP) is commercial. The default solver (SCS) is free but slower. CVXPY enforces DCP rules strictly — your model must be provably convex or it will be rejected.

quick_start.code:
import cvxpy as cp

x = cp.Variable()
prob = cp.Problem(cp.Maximize(x), [x <= 10])
prob.solve()
print(f"Optimal: {x.value}")

solvers.recent_changes:
## CVXPY 1.8.2

This is a patch release with bugfixes and solver interface updates.

### Bugfixes
- Fix a negative axis sum in the Python backends (#3129)
- Fix quad_form_canon silently dropping M1 te
github_recent_prs: Prep for 1.9: DNLP sync
docs: DPP-breaking patterns + decision checklist
Bump github/codeql-action from 4.35.1 to 4.35.2
Use triangular Hessian format when passing QP to HiGHS
Accept INFEASIBLE_OR_UNBOUNDED status in TestCUOPT::test_cuopt_lp_3

solvers.name: Artelys Knitro
solvers.academic_details: Free academic license available for faculty and students at degree-granting institutions.
solvers.description: Leading nonlinear optimization solver, strong on MINLP and NLP
when_to_use: Large-scale nonlinear programming (NLP)
MINLP with smooth nonlinear functions
Need multiple algorithm options (interior point, active set, SQP)
Complementarity problems (MPEC/MPCC)
when_not_to_use: Pure LP/MIP (Gurobi/HiGHS are faster and cheaper)
Global optimization requiring guaranteed global optimum (use BARON)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Commercial license is per-machine. Academic license is generous but requires annual renewal. Knitro finds local optima by default — for global optimization, enable multi-start or use BARON instead. Python interface requires separate license file, not just pip install.

quick_start.install: pip install knitro  # Requires license file
quick_start.code:
from knitro import *

kc = KN_new()
KN_add_vars(kc, 1)
KN_set_var_lobnds(kc, [0.0])
KN_set_var_upbnds(kc, [10.0])
KN_set_obj_goal(kc, KN_OBJGOAL_MAXIMIZE)
KN_add_obj_linear_struct(kc, [0], [1.0])
KN_solve(kc)
KN_free(kc)

solvers.name: Ipopt
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. EPL-2.0. No restrictions.
solvers.description: Interior Point Optimizer for large-scale nonlinear programming
when_to_use: Large-scale nonlinear programming (NLP) with smooth functions
Need a free NLP solver
Continuous nonlinear optimization
Integration with Pyomo, JuMP, or AMPL for NLP
when_not_to_use: Integer variables needed (Ipopt is continuous only — use SCIP or Knitro for MINLP)
Global optimization (Ipopt finds local optima only)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Ipopt is continuous-only — it does NOT handle integer variables. Performance depends heavily on the linear algebra backend: using MUMPS (free) is much slower than HSL MA27/MA57 (free for academics, paid for commercial). Always install HSL routines for best performance.

quick_start.install: pip install ipopt  # or: conda install -c conda-forge cyipopt
quick_start.code:
# Via Pyomo (most common usage):
import pyomo.environ as pyo
m = pyo.ConcreteModel()
m.x = pyo.Var(bounds=(0, 10))
m.obj = pyo.Objective(expr=-(m.x - 5)**2, sense=pyo.maximize)
pyo.SolverFactory('ipopt').solve(m)
print(f"Optimal: {pyo.value(m.x)}")

solvers.recent_changes:
- Fixed call to `getenv_s` on Windows, introduced with 3.14.17.
  An invalid use of this call made the Pardiso interfaces unusable on Windows.

The attached Windows builds include the interface to
github_recent_prs: Fix data-race global variables with atomics
Remove unnecessary <iostream> includes
IpJournalist: Missing override keyword
Draft at NVIDIA cuDSS (GPU sparse linear solver) integration in Ipopt.
Fix rare use-after-free in IpSpralSolverInterface

solvers.name: Timefold (ex-OptaPlanner)
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. Apache 2.0. No restrictions.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Free community edition. Timefold offers paid enterprise support and cloud-hosted version.
solvers.description: AI constraint solver for planning and scheduling, fork of OptaPlanner
when_to_use: Employee scheduling and shift planning
Vehicle routing problems in Java/Kotlin ecosystem
Complex planning problems with many soft constraints
Need incremental score calculation for large problems
when_not_to_use: Pure LP/MIP (use Gurobi/HiGHS — Timefold is metaheuristic-based)
Python-first workflow with small problems (use OR-Tools CP-SAT)
Continuous optimization (Timefold is for discrete/combinatorial problems)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Rebranded from OptaPlanner to Timefold in 2023. Red Hat still maintains a separate OptaPlanner fork. The Python API (timefold-solver) is newer and less mature than the Java API. Enterprise features (multi-threaded solving, real-time planning) require paid license.

quick_start.install: pip install timefold  # or Maven: ai.timefold.solver:timefold-solver-core
quick_start.code:
# Java is the primary API — Python example:
from timefold.solver import SolverFactory
# Requires annotated domain classes
# See docs.timefold.ai for full quickstart

solvers.recent_changes:
We are excited to announce Timefold Solver 2.0.0, the first major version bump in three years. There is a lot to unpack, let's get into it.

## What's new in the Community Edition

### The Neighbo

docs: link to plus enterprise pages
refactor: move incremental back
docs: reference the new license manager
deps: bump the quarkus group with 4 updates

solvers.name: Pyomo
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. BSD-3-Clause. No restrictions.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Free. BSD license allows commercial use.
solvers.description: Python-based algebraic modeling language for optimization. Supports the widest range of problem types of any Python modeling tool.
when_to_use: Python-based optimization modeling with maximum flexibility
Need to switch between solvers easily (supports 20+ solvers)
Complex problem types: GDP, DAE, MPEC, bilevel
Stochastic programming (PySP extension)
when_not_to_use: Simple LP/MIP (PuLP is simpler for beginners)
Convex optimization with DCP verification (use CVXPY)
Need fastest possible model build time (Pyomo has overhead)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: Pyomo is the modeling layer, not a solver. You still need a separate solver (HiGHS, Gurobi, CPLEX, Ipopt, etc.). Some Pyomo extensions (e.g., Pyomo.GDP) require compatible solvers. Model build time can be slow for very large models compared to direct solver API calls.

quick_start.install: pip install pyomo  # Also install a solver: pip install highspy
quick_start.code:
import pyomo.environ as pyo

m = pyo.ConcreteModel()
m.x = pyo.Var(bounds=(0, 10))
m.obj = pyo.Objective(expr=m.x, sense=pyo.maximize)
pyo.SolverFactory('highs').solve(m)
print(f"Optimal: {pyo.value(m.x)}")

solvers.name: JuMP
solvers.academic_details: Fully open source. MPL-2.0. No restrictions.
solvers.description: Julia-based algebraic modeling language for mathematical optimization. Fastest model build times of any AML. Supports the broadest range of problem types.
when_to_use: Julia ecosystem projects
Need fast model generation (JuMP builds models much faster than Pyomo)
SDP or conic optimization (excellent solver interfaces)
Research requiring custom solver extensions
when_not_to_use: Java/.NET deployment (use OR-Tools or direct solver APIs)
Need standalone executable (Julia has large runtime)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: JuMP is the modeling layer — you still need a solver. Julia's package manager makes solver installation easy (Pkg.add("HiGHS")). First-run compilation in Julia can be slow (TTFX), but subsequent runs are fast. Julia ecosystem is smaller than Python's.

quick_start.install:
# In Julia REPL:
# using Pkg; Pkg.add("JuMP"); Pkg.add("HiGHS")

solvers.name: AMPL
solvers.academic_details: Free community edition (limited size). Full academic licenses available. AMPL Community Edition supports up to 500 variables/constraints for free.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact AMPL. Subscription-based. Pricing includes solver access bundles.
solvers.description: Industry-standard algebraic modeling language for optimization. Separates model from data. Supports 50+ solvers.
when_to_use: Need to separate model logic from data (AMPL's core strength)
Access to 50+ solvers through a single interface
Industry/consulting projects with established AMPL workflows
Need AMPL-specific solver options and tuning
when_not_to_use: Simple problems (overhead of AMPL language not justified)
Need tight integration with application code (AMPL is a separate process)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: AMPL Community Edition has 500-variable limit. Commercial AMPL license is separate from solver licenses — you pay for AMPL AND for each solver. AMPL now offers cloud-based (AMPL.com) execution. Python API (amplpy) is available but AMPL language knowledge is still needed.

quick_start.install: pip install amplpy  # Also need AMPL license or community edition
quick_start.code:
from amplpy import AMPL

ampl = AMPL()
ampl.eval('var x >= 0, <= 10;')
ampl.eval('maximize obj: x;')
ampl.option['solver'] = 'highs'
ampl.solve()
print(f"Optimal: {ampl.get_value('x')}")

solvers.name: GAMS
solvers.academic_details: Academic licenses available. GAMS Community License allows small problems for free. Academic pricing is discounted.
solvers.commercial_pricing_note: Contact GAMS. Base license plus per-solver licensing. Can be expensive for full solver suite.
solvers.description: General Algebraic Modeling System for large-scale optimization. Industry standard for economic modeling and operations research. Supports 40+ solvers.
when_to_use: Economic modeling and CGE (Computable General Equilibrium) models
Need access to BARON, CONOPT, or other solvers available primarily through GAMS
Large-scale MINLP via GAMS solver suite
Established GAMS workflows in organization
when_not_to_use: Budget-constrained (GAMS + solver licenses add up quickly)
Simple LP/MIP (overkill — use PuLP or direct solver API)
Modern CI/CD pipelines (GAMS integration can be cumbersome)
solvers.licensing_gotcha: GAMS license is separate from solver licenses — a full GAMS + all-solvers license is very expensive. Community edition has strict size limits. GAMS language has a steep learning curve. Python API (gams-python) is improving but GAMS language knowledge is still needed for advanced use.

quick_start.install: pip install gams  # Requires GAMS installation and license
